**[1:17:30](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4650)** — and I wanna make sure that we set that, you know, set that understanding at the outset that there's some limits to what we'll be discussing here tonight. This is really going to be a general overview to provide the board with some education on 279 administrator contracts. And I think help inform your further discussion as it relates to options in that regard. Alright, so 279 Iowa code chapter 279. It is a monster chapter in the Iowa code. It applies in a lot of different ways. It applies to teacher contracts. What we're talking about here tonight is administrator contracts. So there are some differences as it relates to the protections there. But what 279 essentially requires for administrators

**[1:18:17](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4697)** — is that each administrator in a school district who is given an employment contract has to have certain things in the contract and there are certain provisions and we make sure that those are all included. There's also then mechanisms for governing their operation and enforcement. And from a very basic standard of employment where you might have an at will employee who could be terminated at any time, you know, for no reason whatsoever. You've also got that contrasted with the legal entitlement that perhaps a contract or in this case a statute as well, gives you. And so from a 279 administrator contract, there does need to be a reason to bring that contract

**[1:19:02](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4742)** — to an end. So if the district's wanting to look at, you know, ending, modifying, what have you with regard to administrator contracts, there's certain processes that have to be followed. What I look at is three basic ways for a contract to either be stopped or modified. The first one of course is the administrator can make that change. They can submit a resignation, they can decide that, hey, you know what, I'm not going to continue on for the next school year. So that happens really around this time quite frequently. You know, the spring is a very popular time for people to make a change as they're looking at next year. The code actually requires that administrators give notice of resignation by May 1st, or the date for return of the contract. So, you know, this is always a time

**[1:19:48](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4788)** — when there's a lot of shuffle and movement amongst districts and folks within their districts. The second way is to have a mutual modification. So a mutual modification would be something where the administrator in the district would agree, like any other two people that parties to a contract could agree to say, hey, we're gonna modify a contract and we're gonna replace what we have now with this new one. We're either gonna, you know, change the term, we're gonna change some of the other, you know, provisions in the contract, whatever, whatever it is that the parties agree upon. But they have that power to mutually amend the agreement so that you've got a new form of contract for going forward. And then the third is termination by the district, right?

**[1:20:34](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4834)** — So first you've got the administrator who would have the ability to terminate, you'd have the second mutual agreement between the parties. And then third is the district's ability to either terminate or modify administrator contracts. The reason that that becomes important is that by definition 279 contracts are continuing. So just like our teacher contracts roll over from year to year, administrator contracts will roll over from year to year unless one of those three options is exercised. Either the administrator stops the contract, the administrator and the district agree to do something different, or the district stops the contract. When it comes to the district stopping the contract, there are certain procedures that the code outlines for things to be followed.

**[1:21:20](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4880)** — And oftentimes a district might stop a contract in order to say, hey, we wanna stop what's currently here, but we're gonna replace it with something else. And so that comes up a lot with RIFs, a reduction in force. So you might say, okay, you know what, we don't have the ability to give a full-time administrator contract to, you know, John Smith, but we would give a 0.8 FTE contract to John Smith, but because of the continuing nature of the contract, we might want to terminate the first one and then replace it with the second one. So termination doesn't always mean termination you're done, you're no longer employed. It can also mean some sort of change or modification to the contract that maybe isn't being agreed upon in an amendment or a mutual way.

**[1:22:08](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4928)** — So when you stop that continuing contract, then you either have the option of replacing it with something different or just stopping it altogether. And that, you know, happens from time to time as well. In addition to things like the reduction in force and replacement and all of that. The statutory process for doing that there's a deadline that's May 15th that's in the code. That's something that every district is aware of come this time. It's a little different than it is for teachers. For teachers it's April 30th. And so there's certain things that have to happen by that date in order to initiate the process for termination. Again, whether or not termination means ending contracts or ending employment replacing with a different contract, whatever.

**[1:22:53](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=4973)** — So when you're considering what your options are, right, you know, behooves you to think about either the timeline and what the mechanism is for how you want to accomplish your goals. I would think about, right, kind of putting this into two different buckets. So if you're looking at making changes, again in a general overall sense, you want to think about what is the purpose for the change? What's modifying, you know, the contract, is it going to be motivated, right? By an individual issue, right? Are we looking at modifying someone's individual contract from a termination standpoint or a modification standpoint? If we're doing something based on an individual, then you would probably need to talk about that in a closed session, right?

**[1:23:38](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=5018)** — So you'd wanna talk about a closed session, you'd wanna get that on the agenda. That's not something you can just do, you know, sua sponte, right? You know, at the board table, a closed session has to be posted on the agenda like any other discussion item would be. And then you have to make sure you've met the parameters for the closed session. So if you wanna have that personnel conversation, the parameters for the closed session are that the subject employee is requesting the closed session, the subject employee doesn't have to necessarily attend, it's just that they, you know, would have the opportunity to at least request the closed session. And oftentimes people do attend the closed session and there's some conversation and discussion in advance of making an a decision. The other bucket, right? So that kind of individual personnel driven side.

**[1:24:24](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=5064)** — The other bucket is more like what I would say is like a bargaining situation, more like collective bargaining. That's an exempt session that's different from a closed session. And that you don't need the request of anyone in particular because you're not talking about anyone in particular. You're talking about positions. So closed sessions I think are reserved for, hey, we wanna talk about people and their performance. And an exempt session is we wanna talk about positions of the district and what we're doing for terms and conditions associated with those positions. So, you know, again the best analogy I can think of is a teacher negotiating session where you're talking about a collective group of teachers. And so you might be thinking, you know, okay, with our administrators collectively, is there some sort of change we want to make

**[1:25:13](https://youtu.be/pfpotI660o4?t=5113)** — to the terms and conditions of employment as a group, as a collective, then an exempt session would be appropriate. And again, you wouldn't need the request for any individual because there wouldn't be a performance aspect to it. It would be different. And the reason I encourage one of those buckets to be chosen, or at least to have some consensus, you know, where your motivation is in making modifications and or terminations is that allows you to get into that appropriate session, right? Either get into that closed session and have the conversation or get into that exempt session and have the conversation that's appropriate because that will be the, I think the venue right, by which you're able to actually have a full and frank conversation about what it is you want to do. And then you can come back out into that open session
